fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out that lightweight inference runner we've been talking about. The idea is that our data science folks export a trained model once, and then any production service can just run it without needing the whole training framework installed. We had a similar portability story with that config-loading module from the auth service — same vibe here, just for neural nets.

The runner needs to handle a few different evaluation styles: the standard layered one, a sequence-folding one for time-series stuff, and a more flexible graph-based one for complex routing. Each style reads a self-contained model description and spits out named results as plain text.

One thing that came up in ops review: some of our input pipelines occasionally produce bad values (missing sensors, overflow, that kind of thing) and we need the runner to handle those gracefully rather than blowing up. Also, the output formatting needs to be totally consistent across environments — our diff-based monitoring will flag any whitespace or precision drift as an incident.

Architecture-wise, please don't make this a single giant file — last time that happened with the analytics batch job it was a nightmare to maintain. Keep the parsing, math, evaluation strategies, and I/O handling clearly separated.

Errors should come out in a normalized text format, not raw stack traces or framework exceptions, since downstream log parsers don't know how to handle those.

trigger_keywords: how should numbers be formatted in output
floating point precision in results
what does the numeric output look like
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Output values must be rendered with roughly six significant figures. Trailing zeros and an unnecessary decimal point must be trimmed. Integer-valued results print without a decimal point (e.g. `2`, `-3`, `0`). Non-integer results use the minimal representation (e.g. `0.880797`, `-4.06896e-05`). This exact textual rendering is part of the contract and must be stable across environments.

trigger_keywords: what happens with nan or inf inputs
how are missing or invalid input values handled
default substitution for bad sensor values
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When an input value is non-finite — supplied as the string tokens `nan`, `inf`, or `-inf` — and a default is declared for that variable in the `defaults` map of the config, the engine substitutes the default value before any preprocessing or layer math. If no default is declared, the non-finite value propagates as-is. The substitution happens before the `(value + offset) * scale` affine normalization step.

trigger_keywords: how is each input variable normalized before the network
what preprocessing happens on raw input values
offset and scale transform for inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Every input variable declares an `offset` and a `scale`. Before a value reaches the first layer it is transformed as `(value + offset) * scale`. This applies in all three evaluation modes (feed_forward, recurrent, graph).

trigger_keywords: what error format is used for bad input
how should errors be reported to the caller
normalized error contract for malformed commands
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: All failures must produce a single-line, language-neutral category on stdout. The categories are: malformed JSON → `error=invalid_command` then `detail=malformed_json`; unrecognized mode → `error=unknown_mode` then `mode=<value>`; invalid network description → `error=invalid_configuration`; runtime evaluation failure → `error=evaluation_failed`; output-rank mismatch → `error=output_rank_mismatch`. Never emit a host-runtime stack trace.

trigger_keywords: how does the graph mode print its output
what does scan output look like versus compute
graph output format header and labels
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Graph mode output begins with a header line `<output-node-name>:` followed by value lines. With `op=compute` each value line is `<label> <value>`. With `op=scan` each value line is `<label> <v1> <v2> ...` listing one value per sequence step. feed_forward and recurrent modes print one line per named output as `<name> <value>` with no header.

trigger_keywords: what layer types are supported
which layer architectures does the engine know about
supported layer kinds beyond dense
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The engine must support: `dense` (fully-connected affine + activation), `normalization` (per-feature `weight * (input + bias)`), `maxout` (max over parallel affine sub-layers), `highway` (gated mix of transform branch and carry-through: `H(x)*T(x) + x*(1-T(x))`), `simplernn` (simple recurrent: `activation(W*x + U*h_prev + b)`), `gru` (gated recurrent unit with update gate z, reset gate r, candidate h), and `lstm` (long short-term memory with input gate i, forget gate f, output gate o, cell c). Adding new kinds must not require modifying the core engine.

trigger_keywords: what graph node types are available
which node types can appear in a computation graph
what is a time_distributed node
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Graph nodes can be of type: `input` (reads a named input block and emits a fixed-size vector), `input_sequence` (reads a named sequence input), `feed_forward` (applies a layer by `layer_index` to its single source), `concatenate` (concatenates vectors from multiple source nodes), and `time_distributed` (applies a layer independently at every time step of a sequence source, producing a sequence output for `op=scan`).

trigger_keywords: how should the code be organized across files
what is the expected file structure
is a single file acceptable for the whole engine
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The project must NOT be a single god file. The required separation is: configuration parsing, the core math/graph engine, the evaluation strategies (feed_forward, recurrent, graph), and the I/O adapter (stdin/stdout, command JSON). The core engine must be fully decoupled from stdin/stdout. The adapter alone translates a command into engine calls and renders results. New layer kinds or evaluation strategies must be addable without modifying the core engine.

trigger_keywords: how does the highway layer work exactly
what is the formula for highway gating
carry gate and transform gate in highway layer
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A highway layer computes `output = H(x) * T(x) + x * (1 - T(x))` where `H(x)` is the transform branch (affine + main activation applied to input), `T(x)` is the transform gate (sigmoid of the `t` component's affine output), and the carry branch uses the `carry` component. The identity shortcut `x` is mixed with the transform output using the gate value.

trigger_keywords: how does recurrent mode differ from feed_forward for inputs
sequence input format in recurrent mode
what is the sequence field in recurrent commands
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In `recurrent` mode the command carries a `sequence` field (not `values`). Each key in `sequence` is a named input channel, and the value is an ordered list of scalars — one per time step. The recurrent layer folds the sequence into a single final state vector by stepping through each time step and applying the cell update. The config `inputs` list still declares the offset/scale for each channel.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what values can op take
list all supported op values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'compute', 'scan'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: that compatibility logic from the auth service login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The config-loading and input-preprocessing pipeline: specifically the per-variable `offset`/`scale` affine transform and the `defaults`-based non-finite substitution logic described in Feature 2 (feature2_1_input_scaling.json and feature2_2_default_substitution.json)

context_pointers.vague_reference: the output formatting needs to be totally consistent across environments — our diff-based monitoring will flag any whitespace or precision drift
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The exact numeric rendering contract: ~6 significant figures, trailing zeros trimmed, no unnecessary decimal point for integer-valued results, scientific notation for very small values (e.g. `-4.06896e-05`), as demonstrated in feature4_2_gated_recurrent.json and feature1_dense_activations.json expected outputs

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I can clarify from the business side — you'll want to check the PRD (start.md) and the public test case JSON files in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the exact behavioral contract. The test cases are the ground truth for expected inputs and outputs.

feature1_dense_activations.json.description: Evaluate a single fully-connected layer (identity weights, zero bias) on a two-element input, applying a named element-wise activation to the affine result. Each case selects a different activation so the reader sees how the same pre-activation values (2, -3) are transformed.

feature2_1_input_scaling.json.description: Each named input is preprocessed as (value + offset) * scale before it reaches the network. A linear identity layer exposes the scaled value directly so the preprocessing transform is observable.

feature2_2_default_substitution.json.description: When an input value is non-finite (NaN, +inf, -inf) and a default is declared for that variable, the engine substitutes the default before evaluation. Special inputs are passed as the string tokens nan/inf/-inf.

feature3_1_normalization.json.description: A per-feature normalization layer computes weight * (input + bias) for each element independently (an affine batch-normalization-style rescaling). Two independent features are normalized with distinct weight/bias pairs.

feature3_2_maxout.json.description: A maxout layer holds several parallel affine sub-layers and, per output unit, emits the maximum over all sub-layers' pre-activations. A trailing linear identity layer exposes the selected maximum.

feature3_3_highway.json.description: A highway layer mixes a transformed branch and a carried-through branch using learned transform/carry gates: output = H(x)*T(x) + x*(1 - T(x)). A trailing linear identity layer exposes the gated result.

feature4_1_simple_recurrent.json.description: A simple recurrent layer folds an ordered sequence into a single fixed-size state vector. At each step the new state is activation(W*input + U*prev + b); the engine returns the final state. Input is one named channel sampled over several time steps.

feature4_2_gated_recurrent.json.description: A gated recurrent layer reduces a sequence to a final state using update and reset gates (inner activation) plus a candidate state (main activation). Output is the final two-element state for the supplied single-channel sequence.

feature4_3_lstm_recurrent.json.description: A long short-term memory layer reduces a sequence to a final hidden state using input, forget, and output gates over an internal cell state. Output is the final two-element hidden state for the supplied single-channel sequence.

feature5_1_graph_feedforward.json.description: A computation graph routes a named input node through a feed-forward node to a named output node. The output block is printed as a header line '<output>:' followed by one '<label> <value>' line per output unit.

feature5_2_graph_concatenate.json.description: A computation graph merges two independent input nodes with a concatenate node, then feeds the combined vector to a dense node that sums the two channels. Demonstrates multi-input fan-in within one graph.

feature5_3_graph_multi_output.json.description: A computation graph exposes several output nodes. The caller names which output to evaluate; only that output's header and values are emitted. Two cases select two different outputs from the same graph and input.

feature5_4_graph_sequence_scan.json.description: A computation graph applies the same dense transform independently at every time step of an input sequence (time-distributed), returning a full output sequence. The scan output prints each label followed by one value per time step.
